speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is January 26th, 1997. Survivor is Edith Cimmer, name at birth is Kukes. Interviewer is Alyssa Kotz. It's taking place in Oak Park, Michigan in the United States in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Today's date is January 26th, 1997. My name is Alyssa Kotz and I'm conducting an interview today with Edith Cimmer in Oak Park, Michigan in the US. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, can you please say and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edith Cimmer, E-D-I-T-H C-I-M-M-E-R, Cimmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you please say and spell your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in March the 6th, 1926, in Poland, in city Berezno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-E-Z-N-O, Berezno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you please say and spell your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is, in Hebrew, [HEBREW] Kukes, K-U-K-E-S. Kukes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'd like to begin by asking you a few questions about your hometown, Berezno. Can you talk about Berezno for a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berezno. We-- we were a family from three girls. I was the youngest one. My father-- I didn't know my father. He passed away. I was only nine months old. And my mother was a seamstress. But her family, they were very wealthy in Poland. They owned grocery stores. They owned a factory where they made brick that they build in homes. So they helped us on top of that. And we tried-- you know, my mother worked very hard, tried to-- to make a nice living to raise the children on her own. I was only nine months old when my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a whole story to begin with. My mother was at orphanage. Her parents died very young. And she married. She was maybe 18 years old when she married the first time. And a little girl, my older sister-- she was only two weeks old, and she lived only with my husband-- with her husband three years, and he was the only one son by the parents. And he died very young, and she became a widow with one little child, two weeks old. So his parents wanted her to stay at his house with them together. But when her husband died, she didn't want to stay there. She had no feelings, no interest. So she went to stay with her brother. And her brother was-- he lived in Klesow, K-L-E-S-O-W. My mother's family, they all lived in a different city, not far from Berezno. And she stayed there for a while, and my mother's brother, they had their own five children. So then my uncle-- my mother's uncle, that means my great uncle-- took my mother to his house. My father lived with his first wife for 20 years and they didn't have any children. And he was very unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife died, so he said-- and he was a businessman, like from a lumber yard, that they're building homes [INAUDIBLE]. So when his wife died, he said, what's-- and he was a very, very famous man, very orthodox. So he said, what's the use of my life? When I die, I don't even leave a name, and no one will even know that I existed. He was very unhappy. So suddenly they called somebody start to inter-- introduce my mother. She should marry him. But my mother was very young. She was like in the 20s. And he was-- they said he was already, like, in the late 50s, beginning 60s. He was like to be her father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother didn't want to marry him because he was too old for her. She wanted to suicide herself. She wanted to drown herself because she was very unhappy with him. But the uncle had-- her uncle, her aunt-- had five children of their own, and they couldn't held her anymore with the baby, so she married him. After a year, my middle sister was born, and they named her Yentl, after his father. He was in seventh heaven. A child was born, and they named after his father, Yentl Kukes. And with a year and a half, or close to two years, I was born. My mother got very sick, and she was a whole year in the hospital. My father got sick. She was in one hospital. My father was in a second hospital. And before he got sick, he had to support the three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my first sister was, like, from one mother-- not from, like a-- you know. But we didn't know any difference. My mother didn't teach us to make any difference. We were like three very loving, very devoted and everything. So when I was born, he got very sick. There was no one to raise me. So some people-- Gentile people-- they had their own babies. They put me in their cribs, so their little boy was in one corner and I was in the other corner in the crib. And they used to raise me. And I was very hungry. I was on starvation.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was already seven months old, another lady, Jewish people, a couple, they didn't have any children, they took me there. And they gave me-- so they told me, when I used to grow up, got older there-- they gave me a piece of bread, I ate like a five years old, not like a seven months baby. I was so hungry. And then my father died, and my mother left a widow with three children, small kids. And she was working, and her family helped-- helped her out. And we were-- we were OK. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a struggle financially for your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Financially, her family-- brothers and sisters-- they were very wealthy. Like, one-- one uncle had a bakery, one had a grocery store, one had a factory from brick-- that they made the brick. And they used to help us a lot. So very, very close family. We were, as my mother, we were poor, but we were very nice and polite. We come from up very nice family, you know, strictly orthodox. And the whole family were very nice, polite. So we were OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you lived in your town, what-- did you live mostly amongst Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a neighborhood that-- mixed neighborhood. It was Ukrainian, Polish, and Jewish-- mostly, majority Ukrainian people and Polish and Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship between the Jewish families and their Ukrainian neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very nice. We had nice neighbors. They were-- from the beginning-- they were very, very nice to us. They respected my mother. She-- she loved them very much and we were like-- they respected her very-- very well, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience antisemitism before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, no. We did not. I went to school-- I went to Polish school-- and it used to come-- after Polish school, I went to Heder, to Hebrew-- Hebrew school. And once a weak used to come a Catholic Pope to give an hour, you know, religious day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as Jewish kids, we could have stayed if we wanted. We didn't have to home. So sometimes, we went home. Sometimes, for instance, me, myself, I went home. And sometimes I stayed there in the class. I was interested to hear this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to-- you know, they said the-- the Polish prayer-- [POLISH]. And then they said-- and then once a week, it came the Ukrainian. The-- the-- the Polish, they called [POLISH]. The Ukrainian, they called pope.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned how to say the Ukrainian, the prayer, like [UKRAINIAN]. . I still remember up till now, and that helped me a lot to survive, that-- that [UKRAINIAN], that means the prayer. And should I talk more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were very nice. Went to school. It was a very nice town. It was a small city, not big, but like a 200 Jewish families. But it was very warm and friendly, and my mother were a lovely lady. Everybody respect her very well. She was very friendly, very sociable, very giving. And she taught us kids to be nice, to be polite, to respect people, not to embarrass, and to share whatever we have, no matter how we were poor and everything. So to share whatever, to give charity as much as we can, until Hitler came. So when the war broke out, it was in 1939. I was still in school. It was during the summer. It was vacation time when the war broke out. And they split Poland half-- so half German took up to Bug. That means to the ocean--

speaker: interviewer
text: The River Bug?

speaker: interviewee
text: So half the German were in deep in Poland. And half, I come like more from the Ukrainian side, and half the Russians. So when the Russians came in, in 1939, so it was like Communism. They took all the rich people, the wealthy and everything. They took away their stores. They took away from the rich people everything, and it was like Communism. And then I went to school. So for two years, I went to-- I learned Russian, how to read and write and speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question. What languages did you speak, both in your home and in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, I spoke Jewish and I spoke Polish and I spoke Ukrainian-- three languages. And I spoke Hebrew. I went to Hebrew school. I was about 10, 11 years old, up till the Germans came. I went all the way through. And I learned already, in Hebrew, like [HEBREW]. I knew all the Jewish, you know, history and everything religious. So I spoke fluently Hebrew. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war started, how did the other children in school treat you, or treat the Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, wonderful. We didn't know any-- any different. We had Ukrainian friends. We had Polish friends. We used to associate together. We used to make homework together. We used to sleep at each other houses. And when it came their holidays, it was-- you know, we didn't go to them because we were very religious. We kept strictly kosher and everything. And they knew, so they respected us. I went to synagogue every Saturday with my mother. I had to go to synagogue when it came Yom Kippur. I had to fast. I was 11 years old. I had to fast already because my mother said the children, that they-- they don't have any father, so they don't have any mothers. Children like that, we have to start earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every year, I had to stay with my mother by the-- hold the hand, especially me, because I was the youngest, and to be with her in synagogue every holiday. And every-- and then from the villages, oh, there were a lot of Jewish farmers. They used to come stay in our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: We welcomed them with food and sleep and everything, because they want to go to-- to synagogue for the high holidays, mostly for the Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. Because they didn't have any synagogues in the small villages and the farmers. So we used to have, like, a couple of two families at our house. They slept even on the floor at our house, but they were during the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about your relationship with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sisters, we were very friendly. We were very nice. We never knew what means fights. And my mother-- we loved my mother dearly. When I came from school-- see, here in United-- the life was there very hard, especially in the small towns and the small cities.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to carry water by-- with a pail I used to carry on the shoulders, two pails on the shoulders, like a half a mile from-- how do you call it? A well?

speaker: interviewee
text: From a well. And then we had a cow in the house. We had a barn. We had-- we kept a cow. And we had some chickens. We raised chickens. We raised geeses. We had ducks. And we helped my mother a lot. We washed the floors because she's-- she was constantly busy with the sewing to make-- to make a living. So-- and my older sister, when she was-- before the war-- when she was I think 16 or 17 years old, my mother's sister-- the oldest sister, she had three sons. She didn't have any girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she took my oldest sister to herself and she raised her like her daughter to make my mother easier. So I was just me and my middle sister stayed with my mother together. And my middle sister finished-- finished school and then she went to work. She worked in a restaurant as a cashier by the register, and I still went to school. So how did your life change then when the Russians occupied your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- for us, was OK. But for their-- my fam-- my mother's family was hard because they took off everything and they kicked them out from the house. And a lot of Polish people-- the wealthy, and the Jewish people, the wealthy-- they took everything away. They called them bourgeoisie. That means the rich people, and they sent them to Siberia right away. Those kind of people. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother's family sent to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Us they didn't bother, no. Just families in the city, they were worried that they owned big business and everything, so they took everything away. And then, during the Russian, where we stayed, we used to stay in the line-- [INAUDIBLE] in the line. Like, I got up-- I needed a pair of boots for winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got up around 4:00, 5:00 in the morning and I stayed in the line to get some boots. And when it came up till 12:00, this-- this store, they called a [NON ENGLISH], you know, and they closed already. So I didn't-- I stood for hours when it still was dark, and it came to me, so they closed up. They didn't have any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: The line was for miles and miles. So we had to get everything. We had to stay in the line. It was not the same, like it was the life in Poland before. In Poland was a Democratic life, completely different. The Russian was like Communism, completely different. The groceries or the-- the clothing stores-- so they used to buy a nightgown, and they used to wear, like, a dress. They didn't know that that's a nightgown because in Russia, they didn't have that kind of life like this. So when they came to Poland, they became wild. They saw the luxury and everything, so they became wild.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was everything we had to get. Like, I got up in the morning, went to school. I came from school. I say, it's a line. What kind of line? So I stood on one place on this line, stood for five, 10 minutes. And then I went to another line for five, 10 minutes. So then I find out here, in the line, they're giving bread. In this line, they're giving sugar. On that line, they're giving milk. So that's all we know is to-- whatever we had to get, we had to stay in the line by the Russians, you know, while they occupied. And how long did that last?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was for two years, in 1939 and 1940. And then the Germans came in. A lot of Jewish people from deep Poland, they ran away to us, to-- to our side, to Ukrainian zone. And they said that the German are killing the Jew. It's miserable. But as Jewish people, we did not-- even the Jewish people that they lived deep in Poland, we did not believe them. They used to say that they are lazy to work, or they don't want to work. They did not believe that the Germans are-- hate the Jew. And the gypsies-- first they killed the gypsies right away. They didn't even took them. The minute they found them, right away, they killed them. They were in the first place. And they said, ah, they probably, they're lazy to work. That's why they're running away. They called [NON-ENGLISH], that they're running away from deep Poland to Ukrainian zone, where we used to live. But when the German came to our zone, we thought that we have to work real hard. They're going to make us to work in the field or whatever. But they're not going to take away their lives. They're not going to slaughter us. They're not going to kill us. But we were-- we were mistaken. When they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- I'm sorry. What-- did your mother or your sisters or-- did you ever speak of trying to leave, to go deeper into Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. When they-- my sister, my middle sister-- when they came-- when they, the German, kicked the Russians back to German when they occupied Poland and they went in our zone-- in 1941, they occupied-- and they went already to Russia, to occupied Russia. So they pulled the Russian soldiers deep to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister and a cousin of mine-- my middle sister and a cousin of mine-- they were already on the train to run because the Russians said to us Jewish people, run away! Run away! Don't stay here. You come with us. You come with us to Russia. They're killing the Jewish people. Because they came already from deep from Poland and they saw what they're doing to the Jewish people. So my sister was already on the train to go to Russia. And then in the middle of this, she turned around and she thought to herself, no, I'm going to leave my mother alone, and my sisters, and I should survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they returned back and she came back home. And they killed her. She's not alive. And-- but when they-- when the German came-- so we were still [INAUDIBLE] in the house-- in-- the people, the Jewish people, in their homes, living in their own homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from the beginning, they organized. They took Jewish people like it was-- like a mayor, for instance. And they gave, right away, a notice. Today you have to bring so much and so much silver to them, to the Germans. So whatever, the Jewish-- if not, we're going to come at night. We're going to kill everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the beginning, in 1941, when they occupied the territory. On the second day, you have to bring all the gold that you have. And then you have to bring all the furs that you have. So every-- and they organized, like, about five, six healthy, strong, Jewish men, you know, educated and everything. And they were like the leaders. And they went to the Jewish homes and everything. And we had to come in this and this house. Whatever they had-- whatever we had-- the gold or silver, or the good clothes, or the furs, or whatever, or diamonds, we had to bring it to them. Then they'll-- they'll let us, you know, survive, to live. And we did everything. And then-- and then-- so we did. And then we had to go. So they were-- Ukrainian had already became soldiers, that they helped work with the German. The Polish boys, the young ones, they became-- you know, they volunteered. They worked with Germans. Because the Germans, when they came, they promised them that they were going to give them-- when-- when the Russians came in 1939 and 1940, they took away the land from the people. That everybody had a piece of land and, like in the United States, for instance, they pay taxes and they pay taxes and they had their own.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were wealthy. They were middle class. They were poor class. When the German came in, they promised the Ukrainian people, and they promised the Polish people, that they're going to give them-- it's going to be a democracy again, like it was before. The land that the Russian took over, they're giving them back their land and they can own their own land and their own homes and everything. So they-- So how did-- so how did the rela-- did the Ukrainians then start to treat you differently, the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the beginning, they were nice. From the beginning, they were nice and friendly. But then, when-- when the German came and they were very-- started to be very mean and very nasty and-- and they start to take advantage of the Jewish people. Like the rabbi walked-- first they started that the Jewish people have to wear a yellow on the arm-- on the arm, on the-- on the left arm, like a patch, yellow all around. They should be notified. Wherever they go, they should know that they are Jewish, while we were still in the homes. Then they decided, if you wear, for instance, on the arm, you wear a jacket, then you're covering up. It's no good. So they took off and we had to wear a yellow patch, it's like a [NON ENGLISH], the Jewish star-- a yellow patch in the front, and a yellow patch in the back denoted that we had to know that we are Jewish. And then they started-- the Polish-- they gave notice to the Polish people, [NON ENGLISH]. That means, don't buy anything by the Jew. They closed the grocery stores. They closed the-- the-- the clothing stores. They closed-- they closed all the Jewish stores already. They start already-- and, like, they brainwashed the Polish people. They brainwashed the Ukrainian, the young generation and-- against the Jewish people. They used to write, already, swastikas, you Jude, you Jew, and [NON ENGLISH]. And then they start already punishing the Jew. Like the rabbi walked to synagogue and they took-- they took with a knife and they cut off fingers in the front of everybody. They cut off a ear, and they took with a rope and they put it to-- to the horses into the barn, and they dragged them all over the street, the rabbi. And then another, when they saw-- you know, the-- the Orthodox Jew-- there wore-- they were very religious and they wore the beards, the long beards. And they wore a hat, so they cut them in the middle of the street with knives, and they cut the beard with-- with the skin, with this. And they-- they used to take them and drag them over the street and everybody should see how they-- what they're doing to them with a rope to the-- to the wagon, to the wheel from the wagons. You know, the wagon had, like, four wheels. And they took-- like, one said something, a word that they didn't like it. So they took a gun and they shot him right away. And then they took-- in-- in Europe, the girls, mostly the young-- they took a very bad advantage. When-- when they saw a skin girl, she was, like, sick. A husky girl, a little bit chubby, she's strong. She's healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took-- the Germans, they used to pick up girls and rape them. And cut-- they wore long hair. They were beautiful girls-- gorgeous, nice, good looking, talented, educated. And they used to cut their hair off completely, like a boy. Took advantage. So my sisters, they were-- my-- mostly my middle sister, she was a little bit-- she was husky. She was chubby. And they were afraid a lot of times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of times they hided in the barn and I went to work. I was the youngest, so they didn't touch so much younger people. So they used to take advantage to the police station. And it was raining, pouring. So about five, six-- that's already the Ukrainian boys, they took advantage at the Jewish people. OK. Why don't we pause here. This is the end of tape one.
